narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile includes a solid LSAT score of 37—right above the typical student's 36.7—along with an undergraduate GPA of 2.7, which sits below the average of 3.21. The admissions model evaluated these details alongside your early academic performance to predict your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimating a 68.9% probability that you would fail. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; the school uses this model to prioritize students likely to succeed on the bar and graduate without needing extra attempts. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, which strongly signaled potential struggles ahead. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, thanks to its slight edge over average. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 1—again in the bottom 10% compared to the typical mid-range of around 5.7—further pulled the prediction toward failure, highlighting early academic challenges as a pattern. While your full-time status aligns with most applicants at 92.7%, and your family income is comfortably above average, these didn't outweigh the weight of those low decile rankings in the model's eyes. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early and later school rankings stand out as notably weaker, overriding strengths like your LSAT. This doesn't define your potential, but it explains the cautious decision. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted GPA improvement initiative, and reapply after demonstrating stronger academic momentum. You might also seek feedback from advisors on bolstering weak areas while leaning into your LSAT strength. With focused effort, you can shift this narrative for future applications.
